revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ホーランエンヤ]]
'''Horanenya''' (ホーランエンヤ) is a festival held every New Year in {{ill|Bungotakada, Ōita|en|Bungotakada, Ōita|qid=Q857281|lt=Bungotakada|1=Bungotakada, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, Japan, to pray for bountiful catches and maritime safety. It is designated as a Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Oita Prefecture and is also recognized as an {{ill|Oita Heritage|ja|おおいた遺産|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Heritage|1=Oita Heritage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11261226}}.
[[File:Horan-enya boat.jpg|thumb|Treasure boat (Horanenya)]]
== Overview == A brightly decorated treasure boat adorned with {{ill|Tairyō-bata|en|Tairyō-bata|qid=Q7676389|lt=Large fishing flags|1=Tairyō-bata}}, streamers, and international flags departs from Tamatsu Iso Town, on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Oita)|ja|桂川 (大分県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Oita)|1=Katsura River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11536671}} in Bungotakada City. Young men in {{ill|fundoshi|ja|ふんどし#締め込み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fundoshi|1=Fundoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q976963}} attire board the boat, which first heads downstream to the {{ill|Kotohira-gū|lt=Kotohira-gū|lt=Kotohiragū|ja|琴平宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohiragū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅宮}} (Konpira Shrine), where offerings of mochi are made. The boat then turns upstream toward the {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman-gū (Bungotakada)|lt=Wakamiya Hachiman-gū (Bungotakada)|lt=Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Bungotakada)|ja|若宮八幡神社 (豊後高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Bungotakada)|qq=}}. Rowers chant "Horan Enya Enya Sa no Sassa" as they ride the rising tide upstream.
Mochi in red and white are scattered from the boat, and when spectators on the riverbank offer celebratory gifts, the young men leap into the freezing water to retrieve them.<ref name="godo20130108">[http://www.oita-press.co.jp/localNews/2013_135760602919.html "Horanenya on
13 in Bungotakada City"] Oita Godo Shimbun,
8, 2013</ref><ref name="nishinippon20130114">[http://www.nishinippon.co.jp/nnp/item/343140 "Treasure Boat Brings Luck: Traditional 'Horanenya' in Oita"] Nishinippon Shimbun,
14, 2013</ref>.
While the festival originally took place on {{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's Day|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 元日|1=New Year's Day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q196627}} under the lunar {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tempo Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, the modern schedule is determined based on the timing of high tide.
Similar festivals are also held in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|qid=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Gōtsu, Shimane|en|Gōtsu, Shimane|qid=Q850395|lt=Gōtsu|1=Gōtsu, Shimane}} in Shimane Prefecture, and {{ill|Onomichi|en|Onomichi|qid=Q696694|lt=Onomichi|1=Onomichi}} in Hiroshima Prefecture.<ref>[http://www.nishinippon.co.jp/nnp/item/343051 "Horanenya: An Edo-Era Tradition in Bungotakada City, Oita"] Nishinippon Shimbun,
13, 2013</ref>.
== Origin == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the area now known as Bungotakada was part of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|en|Shimabara Domain|qid=Q1067706|lt=Shimabara Domain|1=Shimabara Domain}}. Tribute rice was transported to Osaka warehouses and Shimabara Domain via cargo ships.
Horanenya is said to have begun in the mid-Edo period as a ceremony to pray for maritime safety and bountiful catches.<ref name="nishinippon20130114" />
The chant "Horan Enya" and the name of the festival are believed to have evolved from the phrases "Horai Eiya" (Treasure Prosperity) or "To Horai, To Horai" (To Paradise).<ref>[http://www.oita-press.co.jp/featureNews/120894237407/2009_12335551018.html "Horanenya: An Oita Heritage"] Oita Godo Shimbun, February 2, 2009</ref>.
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.oita.jp/site/archive/200687.html Horanenya] - Oita Prefecture official page
